Graph_Theory.graph: A graph G consists of a set of vertices V and a set of edges E, where each edge connects two vertices.
Graph_Theory.degree: The degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it.
Graph_Theory.path: A path in a graph is a sequence of vertices connected by edges, where each vertex is distinct.
Graph_Theory.cycle: A cycle is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex.
Graph_Theory.connected graph: A graph is connected if there is a path between every pair of vertices.
Graph_Theory.tree: A tree is a connected, acyclic graph.
Graph_Theory.spanning tree: A spanning tree of a graph G is a subgraph that is a tree and includes all vertices of G.
Graph_Theory.complete graph: A complete graph is a graph where every pair of vertices is connected by an edge.
Graph_Theory.bipartite graph: A bipartite graph is a graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that no two vertices within the same set are adjacent.
Graph_Theory.adjacency matrix: The adjacency matrix of a graph is a matrix where the entry at row i and column j indicates whether there is an edge between vertices i and j.
Graph_Theory.isomorphism: Two graphs are isomorphic if there is a one-to-one correspondence between their vertex sets and edge sets.
Graph_Theory.graph minor: A graph H is a minor of a graph G if H can be obtained from G by deleting vertices, deleting edges, and contracting edges.
Graph_Theory.chromatic number: The chromatic number of a graph is the minimum number of colors needed to color its vertices so that no two adjacent vertices share the same color.
Graph_Theory.planar graph: A graph is planar if it can be drawn on a plane without any edges crossing.
Graph_Theory.clique number: The clique number of a graph is the size of the largest complete subgraph contained in the graph.
Graph_Theory.independent set: An independent set in a graph is a set of vertices no two of which are adjacent.
Graph_Theory.vertex connectivity: The vertex connectivity of a graph is the minimum number of vertices that must be removed to disconnect the remaining graph.
Graph_Theory.edge connectivity: The edge connectivity of a graph is the minimum number of edges that must be removed to disconnect the graph.
Graph_Theory.perfect graph: A graph is perfect if, for every induced subgraph, the chromatic number equals the size of the largest clique.
Graph_Theory.graph homomorphism: A graph homomorphism is a mapping from the vertices of one graph to the vertices of another graph that preserves adjacency.
Graph_Theory.Ramsey number: The Ramsey number R(s,t) is the minimum number of vertices required to ensure that a graph contains a clique of size s or an independent set of size t.
Graph_Theory.oriented graph: An oriented graph is a directed graph with no bidirectional edges (i.e., for every pair of vertices, at most one of the directed edges u → v or v → u is present).
Graph_Theory.strongly connected: A directed graph is strongly connected if there is a directed path from any vertex to every other vertex.
Graph_Theory.weakly connected: A directed graph is weakly connected if replacing all directed edges with undirected edges results in a connected undirected graph.
Graph_Theory.tournament: A tournament is an oriented graph obtained by assigning a direction to each edge of a complete graph.
Graph_Theory.out-degree: The out-degree of a vertex is the number of edges directed away from that vertex.
Graph_Theory.in-degree: The in-degree of a vertex is the number of edges directed towards that vertex.
Graph_Theory.acyclic directed graph (DAG): A directed acyclic graph (DAG) is a directed graph with no directed cycles.
Graph_Theory.transitive closure: The transitive closure of a directed graph is the smallest graph in which, for every directed path from u to v, there is a direct edge from u to v.
Graph_Theory.Hamiltonian digraph: A directed graph is Hamiltonian if there is a directed cycle that visits each vertex exactly once.
Graph_Theory.dominating set in digraphs: A subset D of vertices in a digraph is a dominating set if every vertex not in D is adjacent to at least one vertex in D through a directed edge.
Graph_Theory.Eulerian digraph: A digraph is Eulerian if it contains a closed walk that visits every edge exactly once.
Graph_Theory.semi-Eulerian digraph: A digraph is semi-Eulerian if it contains an open walk that visits every edge exactly once.
Graph_Theory.set: A collection of distinct objects, considered as an object in its own right.
Graph_Theory.relation: A relation between two sets is a collection of ordered pairs containing one object from each set.
Graph_Theory.function: A function from a set X to a set Y assigns exactly one element of Y to each element of X.
Graph_Theory.bijection: A function is a bijection if it is both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto).
Graph_Theory.permutation: A permutation of a set is an arrangement of its members into a sequence or order.
Graph_Theory.combination: A combination is a selection of items from a set without regard to the order of selection.
Graph_Theory.proposition: A declarative sentence that is either true or false.
Graph_Theory.logical connective: Symbols used to connect propositions, such as AND (∧), OR (∨), NOT (¬), and IMPLIES (→).
Graph_Theory.tautology: A statement that is true in every possible interpretation.
Graph_Theory.contradiction: A statement that is false in every possible interpretation.
Graph_Theory.pigeonhole principle: If more items are put into fewer containers, then at least one container must contain more than one item.
Graph_Theory.cardinality: The number of elements in a set.
Graph_Theory.lattice: A partially ordered set in which every pair of elements has both a least upper bound and a greatest lower bound.
Graph_Theory.poset: A partially ordered set, meaning a set equipped with a partial order relation.
Graph_Theory.Boolean algebra: An algebraic structure that captures the properties of logical operations AND, OR, and NOT.
Graph_Theory.generating function: A formal power series in one variable that encodes a sequence of numbers.
Graph_Theory.recursive function: A function that refers to itself during its definition.
Graph_Theory.finite state machine: A mathematical model of computation consisting of states, transitions between states, and actions.
Graph_Theory.monoid: A set equipped with an associative binary operation and an identity element.
Graph_Theory.ring: A set equipped with two binary operations (typically addition and multiplication) that generalizes arithmetic.
Graph_Theory.group: A set with a binary operation that is associative, has an identity element, and every element has an inverse.
Graph_Theory.graph isomorphism: Two graphs are isomorphic if there is a bijection between their vertex sets that preserves adjacency.
Graph_Theory.NP-complete: A class of decision problems for which a solution can be verified in polynomial time, but it is not known if a solution can be found in polynomial time.
Graph_Theory.Turing machine: A theoretical computational model that defines an abstract machine that manipulates symbols on a strip of tape.
Graph_Theory.Algorithm: A step-by-step procedure or formula for solving a problem.
Graph_Theory.Data Structure: A way to organize, manage, and store data efficiently for access and modification.
Graph_Theory.Asymptotic Notation: Mathematical notations to describe the running time or space requirements of algorithms for large input sizes (e.g., Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega).
Graph_Theory.Binary Search Tree (BST): A tree data structure where each node has at most two children, and the left subtree contains nodes with smaller values, while the right subtree contains nodes with larger values.
Graph_Theory.Graph: A collection of vertices (nodes) connected by edges (links), used to model pairwise relations.
Graph_Theory.Heap: A specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property: if A is a parent node of B, then A is ordered with respect to B for a max-heap or min-heap.
Graph_Theory.Dynamic Programming: An algorithmic paradigm that solves problems by breaking them down into simpler subproblems and solving each subproblem only once, storing its solution.
Graph_Theory.Greedy Algorithm: An algorithm that makes the locally optimal choice at each stage with the hope of finding a global optimum.
Graph_Theory.NP-Complete: A class of problems for which no polynomial-time algorithm is known, but a solution can be verified in polynomial time, and any NP problem can be reduced to them.
Graph_Theory.Approximation Algorithm: An algorithm that finds near-optimal solutions to optimization problems where finding the exact solution is computationally expensive or impossible.
Graph_Theory.Randomized Algorithm: An algorithm that employs random numbers to make decisions during execution, often achieving better average-case performance.
Graph_Theory.Persistent Data Structure: A data structure that preserves previous versions of itself when it is modified, allowing access to past states.
Graph_Theory.Red-Black Tree: A balanced binary search tree with nodes colored red or black, ensuring logarithmic time complexity for insertion, deletion, and search.
Graph_Theory.Suffix Array: An array of all suffixes of a string sorted in lexicographical order, used in pattern matching and text indexing.
Graph_Theory.Parameterized Complexity: A framework for analyzing algorithms based on multiple parameters of the input, beyond just its size.
Graph_Theory.Complexity Class P: The set of decision problems that can be solved in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
Graph_Theory.Complexity Class NP: The set of decision problems for which a solution can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
Graph_Theory.VC Dimension: A measure of the capacity of a statistical model or classifier, defined as the largest set of points that can be shattered by the model.
Graph_Theory.2-3 tree: A specialized form of the B-tree where each internal node has either 2 children or 3 children. Key values are ordered to maintain the binary search tree property. The 2-3 tree is always height balanced, and its insert, search, and remove operations all have Θ(logn) cost.
Graph_Theory.80/20 rule: Given a typical application where there is a collection of records and a series of search operations for records, the 80/20 rule is an empirical observation that 80% of the record accessess typically go to 20% of the records. The exact values varies between data collections, and is related to the concept of locality of reference.
Graph_Theory.abstract data type: Abbreviated ADT. The specification of a data type within some language, independent of an implementation. The interface for the ADT is defined in terms of a type and a set of operations on that type. The behavior of each operation is determined by its inputs and outputs. An ADT does not specify how the data type is implemented. These implementation details are hidden from the user of the ADT and protected from outside access, a concept referred to as encapsulation.
Graph_Theory.accept: When a finite automata executes on a string and terminates in an accepting state, it is said to accept the string. The finite automata is said to accept the language that consists of all strings for which the finite automata completes execution in an accepting state.
Graph_Theory.accepting state: Part of the definition of a finite automata is to designate some states as accepting states. If the finite automata executes on an input string and completes the computation in an accepting state, then the machine is said to accept the string.
Graph_Theory.acceptor: In formal languages, any machine whose primary purpose is to determine whether a string is accepted (is recognized to be in a language) or rejected. This is in contrast to a machine that computes some value.
Graph_Theory.activation record: The entity that is stored on the runtime stack during program execution. It stores any active local variable and the return address from which a new subroutine is being called, so that this information can be recovered when the subroutine terminates.
Graph_Theory.acyclic graph: In graph terminology, a graph that contains no cycles.
Graph_Theory.adjacency list: An implementation for a graph that uses an (array-based) list to represent the vertices of the graph, and each vertex is in turn represented by a (linked) list of the vertices that are neighbors.
Graph_Theory.adjacent: Two nodes of a tree or two vertices of a graph are said to be adjacent if they have an edge connecting them. If the edge is directed from a to b, then we say that a is adjacent to b, and b is adjacent from a.
Graph_Theory.adversary: A fictional construct introduced for use in an adversary argument.
Graph_Theory.adversary argument: A type of lower bounds proof for a problem where a (fictional) “adversary” is assumed to control access to an algorithm’s input, and which yields information about that input in such a way that will drive the cost for any proposed algorithm to solve the problem as high as possible. So long as the adversary never gives an answer that conflicts with any previous answer, it is permitted to do whatever necessary to make the algorithm require as much cost as possible.
Graph_Theory.aggregate type: A data type whose members have subparts. For example, a typical database record. Another term for this is composite type.
Graph_Theory.algorithm: A method or a process followed to solve a problem.
Graph_Theory.algorithm analysis: A less formal version of the term asymptotic algorithm analysis, generally used as a synonym for asymptotic analysis.
Graph_Theory.alias: Another name for something. In programming, this usually refers to two references that refer to the same object.
Graph_Theory.all-pairs shortest paths problem: Given a graph with weights or distances on the edges, find the shortest paths between every pair of vertices in the graph. One approach to solving this problem is Floyd’s algorithm, which uses the dynamic programming algorithmic technique.
Graph_Theory.allocated: allocation-Reserving memory for an object in the Heap memory.
Graph_Theory.alphabet: The characters or symbols that strings in a given language may be composed of.
Graph_Theory.alphabet trie: A trie data structure for storing variable-length strings. Level i of the tree corresponds to the letter in position i of the string. The root will have potential branches on each intial letter of string. Thus, all strings starting with “a” will be stored in the “a” branch of the tree. At the second level, such strings will be separated by branching on the second letter.
Graph_Theory.amortized analysis: An algorithm analysis techique that looks at the total cost for a series of operations and amortizes this total cost over the full series. This is as opposed to considering every individual operation to independently have the worst case cost, which might lead to an overestimate for the total cost of the series.
Graph_Theory.amortized cost: The total cost for a series of operations to be used in an amortized analysis.
Graph_Theory.ancestor: In a tree, for a given node A, any node on a path from A up to the root is an ancestor of A.
Graph_Theory.antisymmetric: In set notation, relation R is antisymmetric if whenever aRb and bRa, then a=b, for all a,b∈S.
Graph_Theory.approximation algorithm: An algorthm for an optimization problem that finds a good, but not necessarily cheapest, solution.
Graph_Theory.arm: In the context of an I/O head, this attaches the sensor on the I/O head to the boom.
Graph_Theory.array: A data type that is used to store elements in consecutive memory locations and refers to them by an index.
Graph_Theory.array-based list: An implementation for the list ADT that uses an array to store the list elements. Typical implementations fix the array size at creation of the list, and the overhead is the number of array positions that are presently unused.
Graph_Theory.array-based queue: Analogous to an array-based list, this uses an array to store the elements when implementing the queue ADT.
Graph_Theory.array-based stack: Analogous to an array-based list, this uses an array to store the elements when implementing the stack ADT.
Graph_Theory.ASCII character coding: American Standard Code for Information Interchange. A commonly used method for encoding characters using a binary code. Standard ASCII uses an 8-bit code to represent upper and lower case letters, digits, some punctuation, and some number of non-printing characters (such as carrage return). Now largely replaced by UTF-8 encoding.
Graph_Theory.assembly code: A form of intermediate code created by a compiler that is easy to convert into the final form that the computer can execute. An assembly language is typically a direct mapping of one or a few instructions that the CPU can execute into a mnemonic form that is relatively easy for a human to read.
Graph_Theory.asymptotic algorithm analysis: A more formal term for asymptotic analysis.
Graph_Theory.asymptotic analysis: A method for estimating the efficiency of an algorithm or computer program by identifying its growth rate. Asymptotic analysis also gives a way to define the inherent difficulty of a problem. We frequently use the term algorithm analysis to mean the same thing.
Graph_Theory.attribute: In object-oriented programming, a synonym for data members.
Graph_Theory.automatic variable: A synonym for local variable. When program flow enters and leaves the variable’s scope, automatic variables will be allocated and de-allocated automatically.
Graph_Theory.average case: In algorithm analysis, the average of the costs for all problem instances of a given input size n. If not all problem instances have equal probability of occurring, then average case must be calculated using a weighted average.
Graph_Theory.average seek time: Expected (average) time to perform a seek operation on a disk drive, assuming that the seek is between two randomly selected tracks. This is one of two metrics commonly provided by disk drive vendors for disk drive performance, with the other being track-to-track seek time.
Graph_Theory.AVL Tree: A variant implementation for the BST, which differs from the standard BST in that it uses modified insert and remove methods in order to keep the tree balanced. Similar to a Splay Tree in that it uses the concept of rotations in the insert and remove operations.
Graph_Theory.B∗-tree: A variant on the B+-tree. The B∗tree is identical to the B+tree, except for the rules used to split and merge nodes. Instead of splitting a node in half when it overflows, the B∗tree gives some records to its neighboring sibling, if possible. If the sibling is also full, then these two nodes split into three. Similarly, when a node underflows, it is combined with its two siblings, and the total reduced to two nodes. Thus, the nodes are always at least two thirds full.
Graph_Theory.B+-tree: The most commonly implemented form of B-tree. A B+-tree does not store data at the internal nodes, but instead only stores search key values as direction finders for the purpose of searching through the tree. Only the leaf nodes store a reference to the actual data records.
Graph_Theory.B-tree: A method for indexing a large collection of records. A B-tree is a balanced tree that typically has high branching factor (commonly as much as 100 children per internal node), causing the tree to be very shallow. When stored on disk, the node size is selected to be same as the desired unit of I/O (so some multiple of the disk sector size). This makes it easy to gain access to the record associated with a given search key stored in the tree with few disk accesses. The most commonly implemented variant of the B-tree is the B+-tree.
Graph_Theory.backing storage: In the context of a caching system or buffer pool, backing storage is the relatively large but slower source of data that needs to be cached. For example, in a virtual memory, the disk drive would be the backing storage. In the context of a web browser, the Internet might be considered the backing storage.
Graph_Theory.backtracking: A heuristic for brute-force search of a solution space. It is essentially a depth-first search of the solution space. This can be improved using a branch-and-bounds algorithm.
Graph_Theory.bad reference: A reference is referred to as a bad reference if it is allocated but not initialized.
Graph_Theory.bag: In set notation, a bag is a collection of elements with no order (like a set), but which allows for duplicate-valued elements (unlike a set).
Graph_Theory.balanced tree: A tree where the subtrees meet some criteria for being balanced. Two possibilities are that the tree is height balanced, or that the tree has a roughly equal number of nodes in each subtree.
Graph_Theory.base case: In recursion or proof by induction, the base case is the termination condition. This is a simple input or value that can be solved (or proved in the case of induction) without resorting to a recursive call (or the induction hypothesis).
Graph_Theory.base class: :In object-oriented programming, a class from which another class inherits. The class that inherits is called a subclass.
Graph_Theory.base type: The data type for the elements in a set. For example, the set might consist of the integer values 3, 5, and 7. In this example, the base type is integers.
Graph_Theory.basic operation: Examples of basic operations include inserting a data item into the data structure, deleting a data item from the data structure, and finding a specified data item.
Graph_Theory.best case: In algorithm analysis, the problem instance from among all problem instances for a given input size n that has least cost. Note that the best case is not when n is small, since we are referring to the best from a class of inputs (i.e, we want the best of those inputs of size n).
Graph_Theory.best fit: In a memory manager, best fit is a heuristic for deciding which free block to use when allocating memory from a memory pool. Best fit will always allocate from the smallest free block that is large enough to service the memory request. The rationale is that this will be the method that best preserves large blocks needed for unusually large requests. The disadvantage is that it tends to cause external fragmentation in the form of small, unuseable memory blocks.
Graph_Theory.big-Oh notation: In algorithm analysis, a shorthand notation for describing the upper bound for an algorithm or problem.
Graph_Theory.binary insert sort: A variation on insertion sort where the position of the value being inserted is located by binary search, and then put into place. In normal usage this is not an improvement on standard insertion sort because of the expense of moving many items in the array. But it is directly useful if the cost of comparison is high compared to that of moving an element, or is theoretically useful if we only care to count the cost of comparisons.
Graph_Theory.binary relation: In set theory, a relation defined by a collection of binary tuples.
Graph_Theory.binary search: A standard recursive algorithm for finding the record with a given search key value within a sorted list. It runs in O(logn) time. At each step, look at the middle of the current sublist, and throw away the half of the records whose keys are either too small or too large.
Graph_Theory.binary search tree: A binary tree that imposes the following constraint on its node values: The search key value for any node A must be greater, than the (key) values for all nodes in the left subtree of A, and less than the key values for all nodes in the right subtree of A. Some convention must be adopted if multiple nodes with the same key value are permitted, typically these are required to be in the right subtree.
Graph_Theory.binary search tree property: The defining relationship between the key values for nodes in a BST. All nodes stored in the left subtree of a node whose key value is K have key values less than or equal to K. All nodes stored in the right subtree of a node whose key value is K have key values greater than K.
Graph_Theory.binary tree: A finite set of nodes which is either empty, or else has a root node together two binary trees, called the left and right subtrees, which are disjoint from each other and from the root.
Graph_Theory.binary trie: A binary tree whose structure is that of a trie. Generally this is an implementation for a search tree. This means that the search key values are thought of a binary digits, with the digit in the position corresponding to this a node’s level in the tree indicating a left branch if it is “0”, or a right branch if it is “1”. Examples include the Huffman coding tree and the Bintree.
Graph_Theory.binning: In hashing, binning is a type of hash function. Say we are given keys in the range 0 to 999, and have a hash table of size 10. In this case, a possible hash function might simply divide the key value by 100. Thus, all keys in the range 0 to 99 would hash to slot 0, keys 100 to 199 would hash to slot 1, and so on. In other words, this hash function “bins” the first 100 keys to the first slot, the next 100 keys to the second slot, and so on. This approach tends to make the hash function dependent on the distribution of the high-order bits of the keys.
Graph_Theory.Binsort: A sort that works by taking each record and placing it into a bin based on its value. The bins are then gathered up in order to sort the list. It is generally not practical in this form, but it is the conceptual underpinning of the radix sort.
Graph_Theory.bintree: A spatial data structure in the form of binary trie, typically used to store point data in two or more dimensions. Similar to a PR quadtree except that at each level, it splits one dimension in half. Since many leaf nodes of the PR quadtree will contain no data points, implementation often makes use of the flyweight design pattern.
Graph_Theory.bit vector: An array that stores a single bit at each position. Typically these bits represent Boolean variables associated with a collection of objects, such that the ith bit is the Boolean value for the ith object.
Graph_Theory.block: A unit of storage, usually referring to storage on a disk drive or other peripheral storage device. A block is the basic unit of I/O for that device.
Graph_Theory.Boolean expression: A Boolean expression is comprised of Boolean variables combined using the operators AND (⋅), OR (+), and NOT (to negate Boolean variable xwe write x¯¯¯).
Graph_Theory.Boolean variable: A variable that takes on one of the two values True and False.
Graph_Theory.boom: In the context of an I/O head, is the central structure to which all of the I/O heads are attached. Thus, the all move together during a seek operation.
Graph_Theory.bounding box: A box (usually aligned to the coordinate axes of the reference system) that contains a (potentially complex) object. In graphics and computational geometry, complex objects might be associated with a bounding box for use by algorithms that search for objects in a particular location. The idea is that if the bounding box is not within the area of interest, then neither is the object. Checking the bounding box is cheaper than checking the object, but it does require some time. So if enough objects are not outside the area of interest, this approach will not save time. But if most objects are outside of the area of interest, then checking bounding boxes first can save a lot of time.
Graph_Theory.branch-and-bounds algorithm: :A variation on backtracking that applies to optimization problems. We traverse the solution tree as with backtracking. Proceeding deeper in the solution tree generally requires additional cost. We remember the best-cost solution found so far. If the cost of the current branch in the tree exceeds the best tour cost found so far, then we know to stop pursuing this branch of the tree. At this point we can immediately back up and take another branch.
Graph_Theory.breadth-first search: A graph traversal algorithm. As the name implies, all immediate neighbors for a node are visited before any more-distant nodes are visited. BFS is driven by a queue. A start vertex is placed on the queue. Then, until the queue is empty, a node is taken off the queue, visited, and and then any unvisited neighbors are placed onto the queue.
Graph_Theory.break-even point: The point at which two costs become even when measured as the function of some variable. In particular, used to compare the space requirements of two implementations. For example, when comparing the space requirements of an array-based list implementation versus a linked list implementation, the key issue is how full the list is compared to its capacity limit (for the array-based list). The point where the two representations would have the same space cost is the break-even point. As the list becomes more full beyond this point, the array-based list implementation becomes more space efficent, while as the list becomes less full below this point, the linked list implementation becomes more space efficient.
Graph_Theory.bubble sort: A simple sort that requires Theta(n2) time in best, average, and worst cases. Even an optimized version will normally run slower than insertion sort, so it has little to recommend it.
Graph_Theory.bucket: In bucket hashing, a bucket is a sequence of slots in the hash table that are grouped together.
Graph_Theory.bucket hashing: A method of hashing where multiple slots of the hash table are grouped together to form a bucket. The hash function then either hashes to some bucket, or else it hashes to a home slot in the normal way, but this home slot is part of some bucket. Collision resolution is handled first by attempting to find a free position within the same bucket as the home slot. If the bucket if full, then the record is placed in an overflow bucket.
Graph_Theory.bucket sort: A variation on the Binsort, where each bin is associated with a range of key values. This will require some method of sorting the records placed into each bin.
Graph_Theory.buddy method: In a memory manager, an alternative to using a free block list and a sequential fit method to seach for a suitable free block to service a memory request. Instead, the memory pool is broken down as needed into smaller chunks by splitting it in half repeatedly until the smallest power of 2 that is as big or bigger than the size of the memory request is reached. The name comes from the fact that the binary representation for the start of the block positions only differ by one bit for adjacent blocks of the same size. These are referred to as “buddies” and will be merged together if both are free.
Graph_Theory.buffer: A block of memory, most often in primary storage. The size of a buffer is typically one or a multiple of the basic unit of I/O that is read or written on each access to secondary storage such as a disk drive.
Graph_Theory.buffer passing: An approach to implementing the ADT for a buffer pool, where a pointer to a buffer is passed between the client and the buffer pool. This is in contrast to a message passing approach, it is most likely to be used for long messages or when the message size is always the same as the buffer size, such as when implementing a B-tree.
Graph_Theory.buffer pool: A collection of one or more buffers. The buffer pool is an example of a cache. It is stored in primary storage, and holds data that is expected to be used in the near future. When a data value is requested, the buffer pool is searched first. If the value is found in the buffer pool, then secondary storage need not be accessed. If the value is not found in the buffer pool, then it must be fetched from secondary storage. A number of traditional heuristics have been developed for deciding which data to flush from the buffer pool when new data must be stored, such as least recently used.
Graph_Theory.buffering: A synonym for caching. More specifically, it refers to an arrangement where all accesses to data (such as on a peripheral storage device) must be done in multiples of some minimum unit of storage. On a disk drive, this basic or smallest unit of I/O is a sector. It is called “buffering” because the block of data returned by such an access is stored in a buffer.
Graph_Theory.caching: The concept of keeping selected data in main memory. The goal is to have in main memory the data values that are most likely to be used in the near future. An example of a caching technique is the use of a buffer pool.
Graph_Theory.call stack: Known also as execution stack. A stack that stores the function call sequence and the return address for each function.
Graph_Theory.Cartesian product: For sets, this is another name for the set product.
Graph_Theory.ceiling: Written ⌈x⌉, for real value x the ceiling is the least integer ≥x.
Graph_Theory.child: In a tree, the set of nodes directly pointed to by a node Rare the children of R.
Graph_Theory.circular first fit: In a memory manager, circular first fit is a heuristic for deciding which free block to use when allocating memory from a memory pool. Circular first fit is a minor modification on first fit memory allocation, where the last free block allocated from is remembered, and search for the next suitable free block picks up from there. Like first fit, it has the advantage that it is typically not necessary to look at all free blocks on the free block list to find a suitable free block. And it has the advantage over first fit that it spreads out memory allocations evenly across the free block list. This might help to minimize external fragmentation.
Graph_Theory.circular list: A list ADT implementation variant where the last element of the list provides access to the first element of the list.
Graph_Theory.class: In the object-oriented programming paradigm an ADT and its implementation together make up a class. An instantiation of a class within a program is termed an object.
Graph_Theory.class hierarchy: In object-oriented programming, a set of classes and their interrelationships. One of the classes is the base class, and the others are subclasses that inherit either directly or indirectly from the base class.
Graph_Theory.clause: In a Boolean expression, a clause is one or more literals OR’ed together.
Graph_Theory.client: The user of a service. For example, the object or part of the program that calls a memory manager class is the client of that memory manager. Likewise the class or code that calls a buffer pool.
Graph_Theory.clique: In graph terminology, a clique is a subgraph, defined as any subset U of the graph’s vertices such that every vertex in U has an edge to every other vertex in U. The size of the clique is the number of vertices in the clique.
Graph_Theory.closed: A set is closed over a (binary) operation if, whenever the operation is applied to two members of the set, the result is a member of the set.
Graph_Theory.closed hash system: A hash system where all records are stored in slots of the hash table. This is in contrast to an open hash system.
Graph_Theory.closed-form solution: An algebraic equation with the same value as a summation or recurrence relation. The process of replacing the summation or recurrence with its closed-form solution is known as solving the summation or recurrence.
Graph_Theory.cluster: In file processing, a collection of physically adjacent sectors that define the smallest allowed allocation unit of space to a disk file. The idea of requiring space to be allocated in multiples of sectors is that this will reduce the number of extents required to store the file, which reduces the expected number of seek operations reuquired to process a series of disk accesses to the file. The disadvantage of large cluster size is that it increases internal fragmentation since any space not actually used by the file in the last cluster is wasted.
Graph_Theory.code generation: A phase in a compiler that transforms intermediate code into the final executable form of the code. More generally, this can refer to the process of turning a parse tree (that determines the correctness of the structure of the program) into actual instructions that the computer can execute.
Graph_Theory.code optimization: A phase in a compiler that makes changes in the code (typically assembly code) with the goal of replacing it with a version of the code that will run faster while performing the same computation.
Graph_Theory.cohesion: In object-oriented programming, a term that refers to the degree to which a class has a single well-defined role or responsibility.
Graph_Theory.collision: In a hash system, this refers to the case where two search keys are mapped by the hash function to the same slot in the hash table. This can happen on insertion or search when another record has already been hashed to that slot. In this case, a closed hash system will require a process known as collision resolution to find the location of the desired record.
Graph_Theory.collision resolution: The outcome of a collision resolution policy.
Graph_Theory.collision resolution policy:: In hashing, the process of resolving a collision. Specifically in a closed hash system, this is the process of finding the proper position in a hash table that contains the desired record if the hash function did not return the correct position for that record due to a collision with another record.
Graph_Theory.comparable: The concept that two objects can be compared to determine if they are equal or not, or to determine which one is greater than the other. In set notation, elements x and y of a set are comparable under a given relation R or yRx. To be reliably compared for a greater/lesser relationship, the values being compared must belong to a total order. In programming, the property of a data type such that two elements of the type can be compared to determine if they the same (a weaker version), or which of the two is larger (a stronger version). Comparable is also the name of an interface in Java that asserts a comparable relationship between objects with a class, and .compareTo() is the Comparable interface method that implements the actual comparison between two objects of the class.
Graph_Theory.comparator: A function given as a parameter to a method of a library (or alternatively, a parameter for a C++ template or a Java generic). The comparator function concept provides a generic way encapulates the process of performing a comparison between two objects of a specific type. For example, if we want to write a generic sorting routine, that can handle any record type, we can require that the user of the sorting routine pass in a comparator function to define how records in the collection are to be compared.
Graph_Theory.comparison: The act of comparing two keys or records. For many data types, a comparison has constant time cost. The number of comparisons required is often used as a measure of cost for sorting and searching algorithms.
Graph_Theory.compile-time polymorphism: A form of polymorphism known as Overloading. Overloaded methods have the same names, but different signatures as a method available elsewhere in the class. Compare to run-time polymorphism.
Graph_Theory.compiler: A computer program that reads computer programs and converts them into a form that can be directly excecuted by some form of computer. The major phases in a compiler include lexical analysis, syntax analysis, intermediate code generation, code optimization, and code generation. More broadly, a compiler can be viewed as parsing the program to verify that it is syntactically correct, and then doing code generation to convert the hig-level program into something that the computer can execute.
Graph_Theory.complete binary tree: A binary tree where the nodes are filled in row by row, with the bottom row filled in left to right. Due to this requirement, there is only one tree of n nodes for any value of n. Since storing the records in an array in row order leads to a simple mapping from a node’s position in the array to its parent, siblings, and children, the array representation is most commonly used to implement the complete binary tree. The heap data structure is a complete binary tree with partial ordering constraints on the node values.
Graph_Theory.complete graph:: A graph where every vertex connects to every other vertex.
Graph_Theory.handshaking lemma: In any graph, the sum of the degrees of all vertices is twice the number of edges.
Graph_Theory.Euler's theorem: A connected graph has an Eulerian cycle if and only if every vertex has an even degree.
Graph_Theory.König's theorem: In a bipartite graph, the size of the maximum matching equals the size of the minimum vertex cover.
Graph_Theory.Kruskal's algorithm: Kruskal's algorithm finds a minimum spanning tree by repeatedly adding the shortest edge that doesn't form a cycle.
Graph_Theory.Prim's algorithm: Prim's algorithm finds a minimum spanning tree by starting from any vertex and adding the shortest edge connecting a vertex in the tree to a vertex outside the tree.
Graph_Theory.Hall's marriage theorem: A bipartite graph has a perfect matching if and only if for every subset of one vertex set, the size of its neighborhood is at least as large as the subset.
Graph_Theory.Menger's theorem: In a graph, the minimum number of vertices that must be removed to disconnect two non-adjacent vertices is equal to the maximum number of independent paths between them.
Graph_Theory.Dirac's theorem: A simple graph with n vertices (n ≥ 3) is Hamiltonian if every vertex has degree at least n/2.
Graph_Theory.Brooks' theorem: In a connected graph, the chromatic number is at most Δ + 1, where Δ is the maximum degree, unless the graph is complete or an odd cycle.
Graph_Theory.Turán's theorem: The maximum number of edges in a graph with n vertices and no (k+1)-clique is given by the Turán graph.
Graph_Theory.graph minor theorem: The graph minor theorem states that in any infinite set of finite graphs, one graph is a minor of another. This is a cornerstone of Robertson and Seymour's graph structure theory.
Graph_Theory.Four color theorem: Every planar graph can be colored with at most four colors such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color.
Graph_Theory.Hadwiger's conjecture: If a graph has chromatic number at least k, then it contains a clique minor of size k.
Graph_Theory.Lovász local lemma: For a set of events in a probability space, if each event is independent of all but a limited number of others, then there is a nonzero probability that none of the events occur.
Graph_Theory.Szemerédi's regularity lemma: Every large graph can be approximated by a union of random-like bipartite graphs. This lemma is fundamental in extremal graph theory and theoretical computer science.
Graph_Theory.Erdős–Stone theorem: This theorem determines the asymptotic number of edges in a graph that guarantees the presence of a clique of a given size.
Graph_Theory.Erdős–Rényi model: The Erdős–Rényi model defines a random graph by starting with a set of n vertices and adding edges between each pair of vertices randomly.
Graph_Theory.Tutte's theorem: A graph has a perfect matching if and only if for every subset S of vertices, the number of odd components of the subgraph induced by the removal of S is less than or equal to the size of S.
Graph_Theory.Max-flow min-cut theorem: In a flow network, the maximum flow from a source to a sink is equal to the minimum capacity that, when removed, would disconnect the source from the sink.
Graph_Theory.Alon-Tarsi theorem: This theorem relates the even and odd orientations of a bipartite graph to its chromatic number and is used in proving bounds for the number of certain types of orientations.
Graph_Theory.Expander graphs: An expander graph is a sparse graph that has strong connectivity properties, quantified by expansion parameters.
Graph_Theory.Bollobás's theorem: In random graphs, this theorem describes the phase transition where the graph transitions from being disconnected to connected as the number of edges increases.
Graph_Theory.Menger's theorem (directed): In a directed graph, the minimum number of vertices or edges that must be removed to separate two non-adjacent vertices u and v is equal to the maximum number of disjoint directed paths from u to v.
Graph_Theory.Euler's theorem (directed): A directed graph has an Eulerian cycle if and only if every vertex has the same in-degree as out-degree, and all of its vertices with nonzero degree belong to a single strongly connected component.
Graph_Theory.Tournament theorem: Every strongly connected tournament has a directed Hamiltonian cycle.
Graph_Theory.Ghouila-Houri's theorem: A directed graph is strongly connected if and only if for every partition of the vertex set into non-empty sets A and B, there exists a directed edge from a vertex in A to a vertex in B.
Graph_Theory.Gallai's theorem (directed version): In any oriented graph, the number of vertices can be covered by disjoint paths whose number is equal to the maximum size of an antichain.
Graph_Theory.Dirac's theorem for tournaments: Every tournament with at least three vertices contains a directed Hamiltonian path.
Graph_Theory.Chvátal's theorem (Hamiltonian digraphs): A strongly connected directed graph is Hamiltonian if, for every pair of non-adjacent vertices, the sum of their out-degree and in-degree is at least the total number of vertices.
Graph_Theory.Robbins' theorem: An undirected graph can be oriented to make it strongly connected if and only if the undirected graph is 2-edge-connected.
Graph_Theory.Arc connectivity: A digraph is k-arc-connected if it remains strongly connected after removing fewer than k edges. A digraph with maximum out-degree d is at least d-arc-connected.
Graph_Theory.Transitive tournament theorem: In any tournament, there exists a vertex from which there is a directed path to every other vertex.
Graph_Theory.Acyclic digraph theorem: In any directed acyclic graph (DAG), there exists a topological ordering of the vertices, such that for every directed edge u → v, u comes before v in the ordering.
Graph_Theory.Pigeonhole principle: If n items are put into m containers and n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item.
Graph_Theory.Binomial theorem: (x + y)^n = Σ(k=0 to n) [n choose k] * x^(n-k) * y^k.
Graph_Theory.Inclusion-Exclusion Principle: For two sets A and B, |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| − |A ∩ B|.
Graph_Theory.Handshaking lemma: In any undirected graph, the sum of the degrees of all vertices is equal to twice the number of edges.
Graph_Theory.Euler's theorem for graphs: A connected graph has an Eulerian cycle if and only if every vertex has an even degree.
Graph_Theory.Modular arithmetic theorem: If a ≡ b (mod n), then a and b leave the same remainder when divided by n.
Graph_Theory.Chinese remainder theorem: If n₁, n₂, ..., nₖ are pairwise coprime, then there is a solution to a system of simultaneous congruences modulo n₁, n₂, ..., nₖ.,
Graph_Theory.Lagrange's theorem: In group theory, the order of a subgroup divides the order of the group.
Graph_Theory.Cayley's theorem: Every group is isomorphic to a subgroup of a permutation group.
Graph_Theory.Burnside's lemma: A counting tool that allows one to count the number of orbits of a group action on a set.
Graph_Theory.Euler's totient function theorem: If n is a positive integer and a is an integer coprime to n, then a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n), where φ(n) is the Euler totient function.
Graph_Theory.Zermelo's theorem: Every finite game of perfect information (such as chess) has a winning strategy for one player or ends in a draw.
Graph_Theory.Knaster–Tarski theorem: In lattice theory, any monotone function on a complete lattice has a least fixed point.
Graph_Theory.Fermat's little theorem: If p is a prime number and a is an integer not divisible by p, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p).
Graph_Theory.Cook-Levin theorem: The Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete.
Graph_Theory.Pumping lemma for regular languages: For any regular language, there exists a constant such that any string longer than this constant can be pumped (repeated) in the middle and still be in the language.
Graph_Theory.Master Theorem for Divide-and-Conquer: Let T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d). If log_b(a) < d, then T(n) = O(n^d). If log_b(a) == d, then T(n) = O(n^d log n). If log_b(a) > d, then T(n) = O(n^(log_b(a))).
Graph_Theory.Big-Omega Relation: For a function g(n), f(n) = Ω(g(n)) implies that f(n) grows at least as quickly as g(n) for sufficiently large n.
Graph_Theory.Optimal Substructure Theorem: A problem has optimal substructure if an optimal solution to the problem can be constructed from optimal solutions of its subproblems.
Graph_Theory.Amortized Analysis Theorem: In amortized analysis, the average cost of an operation over a sequence of operations is bounded by an average cost per operation, even if individual operations can have large cost.
Graph_Theory.Binary Heap Property: For a binary heap represented as an array, the parent of the node at index i is located at ⌊(i-1)/2⌋, and its children are located at 2i+1 and 2i+2.
Graph_Theory.Cut Property (Greedy MST Algorithms): In a graph, for any cut that respects the MST, the smallest edge crossing the cut is in the MST.
Graph_Theory.Substitution Method for Recurrence Relat: The substitution method solves recurrences by guessing the form of the solution and using induction to prove it.
Graph_Theory.Dijkstra's Lemma: In Dijkstra's algorithm, once a vertex is extracted from the priority queue, its shortest path distance is finalized and will not change.
Graph_Theory.Chernoff Bound: For independent random variables X_1, X_2, ..., X_n taking values in [0,1], let X = Σ X_i and μ = E[X]. Then, for any δ > 0: P(X ≥ (1+δ)μ) ≤ exp(-δ^2μ/3).
Graph_Theory.Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem: The maximum value of a flow in a network is equal to the capacity of the smallest cut that separates the source and sink.
Graph_Theory.Cook-Levin Theorem: The Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete, meaning every problem in NP can be reduced to SAT in polynomial time.
Graph_Theory.Fermat's Little Theorem in Primality Tes: If p is a prime number and a is an integer not divisible by p, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p).
Graph_Theory.Johnson's Algorithm for All-Pairs Shorte: Given a graph G with possibly negative edge weights, Johnson's algorithm computes shortest paths efficiently using a reweighting scheme.
Graph_Theory.Ellipsoid Method: The ellipsoid method solves linear programming problems by iteratively refining ellipsoids that contain the feasible region.
Graph_Theory.Union-Find Lemma: The amortized time complexity of performing m operations on a disjoint-set data structure with path compression and union by rank is O(m α(n)), where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function.
Graph_Theory.Isoperimetric Inequality for Graphs: For a graph G = (V, E) and a subset S ⊆ V, the edge boundary of S satisfies |∂S| ≥ c|S|(1 - |S|/|V|), where c depends on the graph's structure.
Graph_Theory.Four Russians' Lemma: In boolean matrix multiplication, preprocessing blocks of rows and columns allows reducing the time complexity of multiplication to O(n^3 / log n).
Graph_Theory.Karatsuba's Lemma for Fast Multiplicatio: Using divide-and-conquer, the multiplication of two n-digit numbers can be reduced to three multiplications of n/2-digit numbers, achieving a complexity of O(n^log2(3)).
Graph_Theory.Yao's Lemma: In randomized complexity, the expected cost of the best randomized algorithm for a problem is at least the cost of the best deterministic algorithm for a fixed distribution over inputs.
